enter.speaker: narrator
enter.text: Heat rolls from the forge in waves. The air tastes of iron and charcoal. A broad man stands at the anvil, hammer resting on his shoulder, watching you with the patience of someone who measures time in blade-lengths.

hello.speaker: kael
hello.text: You need something made, or you need something fixed. Either way, bring materials and coin. The anvil does the rest.

choices.text: There is a piece on your bench you do not finish.
I brought the materials. Iron, bone, moonpetal.
I laid the sword on her grave.
Your weapons are different now, Kael.
Have you anything you would not show another smith?
How did you learn to smith?
Tell me about the weapons you've made.
What do you need that's hard to come by?

iron-offer.speaker: kael
iron-offer.text: The supply wagons stopped coming. I have coal, I have skill, but the ore bin is empty. Three pieces of iron ore — from the mines, from the depths, from wherever you find it. Bring it here and I can keep this forge running.

iron-accept.speaker: kael
iron-accept.text: Raw ore, not slag. I can tell the difference.

iron-turnin.speaker: kael
iron-turnin.text: Three pieces. Good weight, clean grain. This will keep the forge lit for a while.

iron-done.speaker: kael
iron-done.text: Forty-five gold. And the next blade I make, I will give it an edge that remembers who brought the iron. Come back when you need steel.

craft-info.speaker: kael
craft-info.text: Blades, mail, potions if you bring the petals. Check the anvil — it knows what I can do better than I can explain it.

materials.speaker: kael
materials.text: Iron from the mines past Greenhollow. Pelts and bone from whatever is trying to kill you. Moonpetals grow where the forest thins. Bring them here. I do not go looking for trouble — I shape what trouble leaves behind.

kael-craft-lore.speaker: kael
kael-craft-lore.text: My father was a farrier. Shod horses, fixed axles, bent the odd hinge. I watched him work until I understood the iron, not just the shape he was pushing it into. There is a point in every piece where the metal tells you what it wants to be.

kael-craft-lore-2.speaker: kael
kael-craft-lore-2.text: Most smiths fight that. I stopped fighting it. My blades hold better for it. Ask anyone who has carried one long enough.

kael-weapons-lore.speaker: kael
kael-weapons-lore.text: Made a blade once for a woman who wanted her name in the crossguard — Elven characters, she said, for 'patience.' Took me three days to get the inlay right. She tested it on the anvil corner and walked out without a word.

kael-weapons-lore-2.speaker: kael
kael-weapons-lore-2.text: She came back six months later with a nick in the edge and a pouch of silver. Said nothing about the nick, just 'fix it.' I did. Some clients you remember. She is one.

kael-needs.speaker: kael
kael-needs.text: Wraith dust. I know how that sounds. There is a process — pack it into the steel during folding, quench it at the right moment. The blade comes out darker, harder, holds an edge longer than anything I can make with iron alone.

kael-needs-2.speaker: kael
kael-needs-2.text: I have not had a supply in months. If you find any in the depths, bring it here before you sell it to someone who does not know what to do with it.

leave.speaker: kael
leave.text: The forge stays hot. Come back when you have something worth hammering.

hire-kael.speaker: kael
hire-kael.text: Seventy-five gold. I do not work cheap, and I do not die easy. You get a man who takes hits that would drop most. That is worth the price.

hire-kael-confirm.speaker: kael
hire-kael-confirm.text: Right. Let me bank the forge and get my hammer. Lead on.

kael-backstory-1.speaker: kael
kael-backstory-1.text: My father was a smith in the village the Hollow King destroyed. He did not run. He forged until the roof came down. Every blade I make carries a piece of what he taught me. The masterwork pieces... those are the ones I make when I cannot sleep.

kael-backstory-1-close.speaker: kael
kael-backstory-1-close.text: You killed the thing that killed his village. I cannot repay that. But every weapon I make for you from now on — I will make it like he would have. That is the best I have.

kael-rank-piece.speaker: kael
kael-rank-piece.text: B-rank, the seal says. That changes the conversation. There is a piece I keep wrapped in oilcloth under the anvil. I have not finished it. I have not started it, properly. Most days I tell myself I will not.

kael-rank-piece-2.speaker: kael
kael-rank-piece-2.text: Bring me a fragment of star-iron — the kind that fell east of Ashfields the year the sky cracked. I will know it when I see it. If you carry one in here, I will finish the piece. It will not be cheap. It will not be quick. But it will be yours alone.

forge-offer.speaker: narrator
forge-offer.text: Kael's hammer pauses. He sets it down on the anvil with a small precise sound and reaches under the bench for an oilcloth bundle. He lays it on the counter and unwraps it: a sword, mid-build. The blade is half-shaped, the tang bare, the crossguard not yet inlaid. It is the only unfinished piece on the wall.

forge-offer-2.speaker: kael
forge-offer-2.text: Roa. Eighteen years old. Came in with her own coin and her own design — a hand-and-a-half, lighter than her arm wanted, balanced where her wrist liked it. She walked north before the moonpetal was in. She did not walk back.

forge-offer-3.speaker: kael
forge-offer-3.text: I held off finishing it. Two summers now. I am not a man who lets work sit. The blade does not let me sleep. Bring me what she paid for: two iron ore, one bone shard, one moonpetal. I will finish the piece. After that — there is one more thing I will ask.

forge-accept.speaker: kael
forge-accept.text: Plain materials. Nothing special. The honoring is in the finishing of it, not the price of the metal. Go.

forge-materials-turnin.speaker: narrator
forge-materials-turnin.text: Kael takes the materials one at a time. The iron weighs fair. The bone shard he turns once between his fingers — a habit, not an inspection. The moonpetal he sets aside on a square of clean linen. He does not say anything for a long moment.

forge-materials-turnin-2.speaker: kael
forge-materials-turnin-2.text: Come back at dusk. I will work it through. The blade does not need an audience for the hammering — the rest is between me and the metal.

forge-materials-turnin-3.speaker: kael
forge-materials-turnin-3.text: When you return: take her sword. Lay it on her grave in Mossbarrow Cairn — the stone with the rough cut at the top, second row from the path. Do not bury it. She wanted it carried. Carry it the last step of the way.

forge-grave-done.speaker: narrator
forge-grave-done.text: Kael does not ask. He has been watching the door since dusk. When you walk in empty-handed, he reads it in your shoulders.

forge-grave-done-2.speaker: kael
forge-grave-done-2.text: Aye. The blade is where it should be. I will not go look at it. The going would not be for her — it would be for me. The piece stays where you put it.

forge-grave-done-3.speaker: kael
forge-grave-done-3.text: Three hundred and eighty gold. Five hundred and forty experience. Two summers of unfinished work, closed in three nights and one walk to the cairn. Take the coin and do not refuse it. The forge will run cleaner for the rest of the year.
